message.content: Adversaries may compromise third-party Virtual Private Servers (VPSs) to use as infrastructure for their operations, leveraging the ubiquity and trust of reputable cloud service providers. This tactic makes it challenging to trace activities back to the adversaries. By exploiting compromised VPSs, adversaries can conduct various stages of their lifecycle, such as Command and Control (C2), while benefiting from the higher reputation of the cloud service providers and the compromised third-party. For instance, the Turla group has utilized VPS infrastructure from compromised Iranian threat actors. The potential impact includes obfuscation of the adversary's origin and enhanced persistence. Common exploitation methods involve gaining unauthorized access to third-party VPSs through vulnerabilities or stolen credentials. Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual access patterns, implementing strict access controls, regularly updating and patching systems, and employing network segmentation to limit the impact of a compromised VPS.
